did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the YOLOv4 deep learning object detection algorithm to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions into three cancer types: basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma. The study involves training the model on a hybrid dataset from ISIC and DermNet NZ repositories and evaluating performance using mean average precision (mAP).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work using the YOLOv4 deep learning algorithm to classify skin lesions into three cancer types (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma). It details the methodology including dataset creation from ISIC and DermNet NZ, manual annotation, model training for 6000 epochs, and reports specific performance metrics such as mean average precision (mAP) and recall. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICACCS), it contains original results and technical implementation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Neural Nets in Real-Time Detection of Cancerous Epidermal Lesions
journal: 2022 8th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
brief_summary: This study addresses the real-time detection and classification of three common skin cancer types: basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma. The authors utilized the YOLOv4 deep learning object detection algorithm to analyze dermoscopic images. The dataset was constructed from the ISIC archive and DermNet NZ repositories, comprising 250 manually annotated images per class, split into training and validation sets. The model was trained for 6000 epochs, with performance evaluated using mean average precision (mAP) and recall metrics. The results indicated that the YOLOv4 model achieved a mean average precision of 78.02% and recall values ranging from 0.72 to 0.80 across the cancer classes. The study concludes that YOLOv4 is accurate enough for potential integration with dermoscopy IoT devices for real-time clinical assistance.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary classes used in the YOLOv4 object detection model for real-time classification. Dataset included 250 images (225 training, 25 validation) sourced from ISIC archive.

One of the three primary classes used in the YOLOv4 object detection model. A hybrid dataset was created for this class because the mAP from ISIC alone was not satisfactory, combining images from ISIC and DermNet NZ repositories (250 images total: 225 training, 25 validation).
One of the three primary classes used in the YOLOv4 object detection model. Dataset included 250 images (225 training, 25 validation) sourced from ISIC archive.

all_task_types.target: Bounding box coordinates and class label for skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper uses the YOLOv4 object detection algorithm to predict bounding boxes and classify dermoscopic images into one of three discrete cancer types: basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma. Performance is evaluated using mean average precision (mAP) and recall, which are standard metrics for object detection tasks involving classification.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The model performs a triple-class classification problem to identify the specific cancer subtype within the detected lesion. The output includes class probabilities for these discrete categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A public repository of skin lesion images used to gather images for Basal Cell Carcinoma and Melanoma classes. For Squamous Cell Carcinoma, images were sourced from this archive as part of a hybrid dataset.

use.training: Images were downloaded in .jpeg format, manually annotated using LabelImg, and split into a 9:1 ratio for training (225 images per class).
use.validation: Images were split into a 9:1 ratio for validation (25 images per class).
use.testing: The best weights were tested on a test set containing 25 images of each class (75 total).
use.other: Used as a primary source for Basal Cell Carcinoma and Melanoma; combined with DermNet NZ for Squamous Cell Carcinoma.

datasets_used.name: DermNet NZ
datasets_used.description: A public image dataset of dermoscopic images. It was used to create a hybrid dataset for Squamous Cell Carcinoma because the mAP from ISIC alone was not satisfactory.

use.training: Images were combined with ISIC images to form a hybrid dataset of 250 images for Squamous Cell Carcinoma, which was then split 9:1 for training.
use.validation: Images were combined with ISIC images to form a hybrid dataset of 250 images for Squamous Cell Carcinoma, which was then split 9:1 for validation.
use.other: Used to supplement ISIC data for the Squamous Cell Carcinoma class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using deterministic metrics such as mean average precision (mAP) and recall.

ISIC_and_DermNet_NZ_Hybrid_Dataset.split: Test Set (75 images total: 25 per class)
ISIC_and_DermNet_NZ_Hybrid_Dataset.notes: The model was trained for 6000 epochs. The best weights were selected based on mean average precision (mAP) at an Intersection over Union (IoU) threshold of 0.5. The overall mean average precision was 78.02%. Recall values ranged from 0.72 to 0.80 across the three cancer classes.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; model trained from scratch on the custom hybrid dataset (ISIC and DermNet NZ) for 6000 epochs.

architecture_details.implementation: Cloned Darknet repository with OpenCV and GPU acceleration enabled
architecture_details.backbone: Not explicitly specified (standard YOLOv4 backbone implied)
architecture_details.heads: Single neural network for simultaneous bounding box prediction and class probability
architecture_details.classes: 3 (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)

preprocessing_techniques: Manual annotation of images using LabelImg software to create bounding boxes and class labels
Dataset splitting into training (90%) and validation (10%) sets
Hybrid dataset creation by combining images from ISIC Archive and DermNet NZ repositories for Squamous Cell Carcinoma class
conclusions: The YOLOv4 deep learning object detection algorithm is accurate in detecting and classifying skin cancer subtypes (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma).
The YOLOv4 model achieved a mean average precision (mAP) of 78.02% and recall values ranging from 0.72 to 0.80 across the three cancer classes.
The YOLOv4 algorithm can be integrated with dermoscopy IoT devices for real-time cancer detection by dermatologists.
Creating a hybrid dataset combining images from the ISIC archive and DermNet NZ repositories was necessary to achieve satisfactory mAP for squamous cell carcinoma classification.

The study is the first of its kind to classify lesions into the three most common skin cancer types (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma) using object detection, whereas previous work primarily focused on binary classification (benign vs. malignant).
important_remarks: The study uses a very small dataset (750 total images, 225 per class for training) which limits the generalizability and robustness of the deep learning model.

The dataset for Squamous Cell Carcinoma is a hybrid combination of ISIC and DermNet NZ repositories because the mAP from ISIC alone was not satisfactory, indicating potential data leakage or bias if sources are not strictly separated.

No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on a held-out test set from the same source repositories (ISIC/DermNet NZ), raising concerns about overfitting to specific data distributions.

The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the performance metrics (mAP and recall), making it difficult to assess the statistical significance or variability of the results.

The model was trained for 6000 epochs with weight saving every 1000 epochs, but the specific epoch selected for final testing is not explicitly justified beyond having the 'best mAP', which may lead to selection bias.

The study claims to be the first to classify lesions into three specific cancer types using object detection, but this claim is based on a limited literature review and may overlook other multi-class classification studies.
The paper does not discuss potential biases in the ISIC and DermNet NZ datasets (e.g., demographic, geographic, or equipment biases) that could affect real-world clinical deployment.
The recall for Melanoma and Squamous Cell Carcinoma is relatively low (0.72), which is a critical limitation for cancer detection where false negatives can have severe clinical consequences.
The study does not address class imbalance issues, although the dataset appears balanced (250 images per class), the underlying prevalence of these cancers in the real world is highly imbalanced.
The paper does not report computational efficiency metrics (e.g., inference time per frame) despite claiming real-time detection capability, which is essential for IoT device integration.
